# Implementation Plan: Codex Platform Support

**Branch**: `004-codex-support` | **Date**: 2026-04-07 | **Spec**: `/specs/004-codex-support/spec.md`
**Input**: Feature specification from `/specs/004-codex-support/spec.md`

**Note**: This plan is generated by the `/speckit.plan` command. See `.specify/templates/plan-template.md` for the execution workflow.

## Summary

Extend myusage-skill to ingest Codex session data from `~/.codex/state_N.sqlite` and integrate it into the generated usage report alongside existing Claude Code and Copilot data. Codex sessions are discovered by reading the session database, extracting metadata, resolving active model names from rollout JSONL files, and applying the same session categorization and character classification logic used for other platforms. Codex will appear as a unified platform entry in all report sections (platform breakdown, activity timeline, category distribution) with graceful handling when the database is absent or corrupted.

## Technical Context

**Language/Version**: Python 3.10+ (stdlib only per constitution)  
**Primary Dependencies**: None (stdlib only — includes sqlite3, json, pathlib)  
**Storage**: SQLite (read-only) — `~/.codex/state_N.sqlite` + JSONL rollout files  
**Testing**: unittest (stdlib, already in use for evals)  
**Target Platform**: Linux, macOS, Windows (any platform with Codex CLI/VS Code)  
**Project Type**: CLI skill (report generator)  
**Performance Goals**: Report generation completes in same wall-clock time as pre-Codex (no measurable regression for <500 sessions)  
**Constraints**: Zero new runtime dependencies; Codex data is strictly read-only; graceful skip if database missing/corrupted  
**Scale/Scope**: Support typical Codex user session volumes (typical range: 10–1000 sessions over months/years)

## Constitution Check

*GATE: Must pass before Phase 0 research. Re-check after Phase 1 design.*

- [x] **Evals-First**: Evals are planned for every user story (EVAL-001 through EVAL-005 defined in spec.md).
- [x] **Agent Agnostic**: Design uses neutral terminology ("tool", "platform", "session"). No agent-specific syntax.
- [x] **Zero Dependencies**: Only stdlib (sqlite3, json, pathlib). No new pip dependencies. Chart.js approved inlined asset exception.
- [x] **Simplicity**: Reuse existing session categorization and character classification logic. Minimal new data structures.
- [x] **Trunk-Based**: This is a spec branch. Implementation will follow on `004-codex-support-impl`.
- [x] **LLM-Agnostic Insights**: Codex identified as a data source; report labels remain neutral (no vendor names hardcoded in generator). Model names derived from Codex data, not assumed.

## Project Structure

### Documentation (this feature)

```text
specs/004-codex-support/
├── plan.md              # This file (/speckit.plan command output)
├── research.md          # Phase 0 output (/speckit.plan command)
├── data-model.md        # Phase 1 output (/speckit.plan command)
├── quickstart.md        # Phase 1 output (/speckit.plan command)
├── contracts/           # Phase 1 output (/speckit.plan command) — if needed
└── tasks.md             # Phase 2 output (/speckit.tasks command)
```

### Source Code (repository root)

```text
scripts/
└── generate_report.py          # Report generator (enhanced with Codex parsing)

evals/
├── evals.json                   # Eval definitions (add Codex evals: EVAL-001 through EVAL-005)
└── fixtures/
    ├── claude_code/             # Existing
    ├── copilot_vscode/          # Existing
    ├── copilot_cli/             # Existing
    └── codex/                   # NEW: Codex session fixtures (3+ synthetic sessions)
        ├── state_1.sqlite       # SQLite database with threads table
        └── rollouts/            # JSONL rollout files referenced by threads
```

**Structure Decision**: Single project structure. The skill is a CLI report generator with no frontend/backend split. Codex support is added as a new data source module within `generate_report.py`, mirroring the structure for existing platforms (Claude Code, Copilot CLI, Copilot VS Code). No new dependencies or file structure required.
